food_subgroup: Non-alcoholic carbonated beverages with fresh citrus juice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fresh lime soda. FOODON_03301710 (carbonated beverage) is the closest parent category. A more specific term combining fresh citrus juice and carbonated water does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0AN (Soft drinks and similar beverages) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code for fresh lime soda with carbonated water exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 (Carbonated soft drink) is the closest parent. Fresh lime soda is a minimally processed variant using fresh lime juice rather than flavouring syrup.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of fresh lime soda is approximately 250 ml, consisting of freshly squeezed lime juice mixed with chilled carbonated water, typically served with ice.

nutrient_source.title: Lime juice, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167659); Carbonated water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831); composite estimate for fresh lime soda (approximately 15% lime juice, 85% carbonated water)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for fresh lime soda exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy components: raw lime juice (FDC ID 167659) at approximately 15% by volume and carbonated water (FDC ID 174831) at approximately 85% by volume, reflecting a standard Indian restaurant preparation. Sugar addition is optional and not included in the base estimate; sodium reflects trace mineral content of carbonated water. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate are derived from the lime juice fraction only, as carbonated water contributes negligible macronutrients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; lemon/lime juice and carbonated water analogy
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fresh lime soda. Lime juice has negligible glycemic carbohydrates and a GI effectively near zero. Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic convention, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) as this beverage contains no meaningful glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is low. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (1 × 1.3 × 250/100) / 100 ≈ 0.3, rounded to one decimal place. Analogous evidence drawn from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, which confirm citrus juices have low GI values (GI ≤ 40) and that diluted citrus-carbonated beverages without added sugar have negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~4 mg per 100 g); potassium from lime juice may support blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Fresh lime soda without added salt is a low-sodium beverage suitable for individuals with hypertension; patients should request the unsalted version as restaurant preparations may add salt or black salt (kala namak), which would significantly increase sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic load when prepared without added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fresh lime soda prepared without added sugar has a negligible glycemic load (GL ≈ 0.3 per 250 ml serving) and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; patients should specify no added sugar as sweetened versions can contain 20–30 g of sugar per serving, substantially raising postprandial glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and cholesterol; vitamin C from lime juice provides antioxidant benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fresh lime soda is essentially fat-free and cholesterol-free, making it a heart-friendly beverage choice; the vitamin C content from fresh lime juice (~12.5 mg per 250 ml serving) contributes modest antioxidant support, though patients should avoid sweetened or salted versions that may add cardiovascular risk factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh Lime Soda is a popular Indian non-alcoholic beverage made by combining freshly squeezed lime juice with chilled carbonated water (soda water), typically served over ice. It is a staple refreshment at Indian restaurants across South Asia and in the Indian diaspora communities of Singapore, Malaysia, and the United Kingdom. The drink is characteristically tart, effervescent, and refreshing, with a bright citrus flavour derived from the fresh lime juice. It may be served sweet (with sugar syrup), salted (with black salt or regular salt), or plain. The base preparation without added sugar or salt is extremely low in calories, providing approximately 4.5 kcal per 100 g, with negligible fat and protein. Carbohydrate content is minimal at approximately 1.3 g per 100 g, derived entirely from the natural sugars in lime juice. The beverage provides a small amount of vitamin C (~5 mg per 100 g) and potassium (~22 mg per 100 g) from the lime juice fraction. Its glycemic index is effectively zero, making it one of the most diabetes-friendly beverage options available at Indian restaurants. Clinically, the unsweetened and unsalted version is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, it is commonly found on the menus of Indian and Indian-fusion restaurants and is sometimes listed as Nimbu Soda or Lime Soda. The drink is vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal.
common_user_queries: Is fresh lime soda good for diabetics?
How many calories in fresh lime soda?
Can I drink lime soda if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of lime soda?
Difference between sweet and salted lime soda

confidence_reason: No direct entry for fresh lime soda exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for raw lime juice and carbonated water at a standard restaurant dilution ratio (~15% lime juice, ~85% carbonated water). The estimation is methodologically sound but not derived from a direct measurement of the composite dish. GI is assigned by convention (value 1) as the beverage contains no meaningful glycemic carbohydrates, supported by analogous evidence from Atkinson et al. 2021.
notes: Fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — raw lime juice contains negligible fat per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167659), and carbonated water contains no fat; fat_g is naturally zero for this beverage. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are all 0.0: confirmed zero by the same source rationale. Protein_g is 0.1 g per 100 g, reflecting the trace protein in the lime juice fraction; this is not zero. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are all -1 as these micronutrients are not reliably reported for the composite fresh lime soda preparation in the USDA SR Legacy source at the dilution ratio used; values are below the reporting threshold or not disaggregated for this beverage form. Glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is low per convention for zero-GI beverages. Glycemic load per 250 g serving is 0.3 (calculated as GI 1 × carbohydrate_g 1.3 × serving_g 250 / 100 / 100). Nutrient values reflect the unsweetened, unsalted base preparation; sweetened or salted versions served in restaurants will have materially different sodium and sugar profiles. Price range SGD 5.50–8.00 preserved from field survey data (2 restaurants: Mr Tandoor – Boat Quay and Fiesta Mexico – Three Amigos Indian and Mexican Restaurant). Cuisine tagged as Indian per primary restaurant context; the beverage is pan-South Asian in origin.

Documented fat_g = 0.0 and all other zero macros with 'confirmed zero' and 'naturally zero' justification phrases in notes.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes with explicit reason (not reported in source at this dilution ratio).
Used only permitted ontology match_type values: 'exact', 'closest_parent', 'none'.
